item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Such a different take on sarkism, so glad random scp brought me here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel sorry for the Karcist; good deeds really do never go unpunished. +1

item.title: Update
item.content:encoded:
Thank you all for reading my article! It's been a while, but I finally updated it to remove a bunch of boring information and to fix up a plot hole. Shoutout to glewmie for looking over the updated version.

I'd also like to say that even though I don't reply to comments much, I do read them all, and I appreciate the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Temporary -1, because several users have noticed a pretty glaring plot hole which hasn't been addressed by the author. Once that's fixed, happy to change to +1. I think the concept and writing are very good, and I enjoyed the depth and detail you put into this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is suspected, but unconfirmed, that SCP-4140-2 has also expanded considerably into the surrounding rock walls.

What, you can't harmonize and play a few ground-penetrating sonars in time with the music?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If I must live forever in the dark so that all others may see the glorious light of Ikunaan, so be it.

This guy gets it.

Amazing article, big +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was just made aware of this. Great job! It really pleases me to see such fine new additions to the Sarkic Cults :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love Proto-Sarkism and variations thereof, as they end up being less of "evil flesh villains" and more of a legitimate belief system made up of regular, albeit slightly fucked up, people who just want to live their lives and make it to paradise. The Karcist's sacrifice at the end is a noble one, and the scrolls correctly portray their rightful fear of Yaldabaoth and his Archons. These are the same beings who attempted to murder the Prophet Ion with their trials because they feared his power, and did not succeed. Similarly, Yaldabaoth is unknowing and uncaring. He doesn't think. He just consumes. Such a god is not to be worshipped, but feared and contained.

I found myself empathizing with the Karcist here, and I love the work that this does in building up Sarkic and Daevite lore. Their history, as well as the war with the Mekhanists and Greeks leading up to the Bronze Age Collapse, are amongst my favorite subjects on this wiki. I hope to see much more from you, because this is fantastic! Enthusiastic +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love Karcist scips, and this had me reading until the end. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the last couple sentences are meant to imply that, yes, there were remaining third-stage instances made & sustained by the Karcist, but a cave-in killed them off at some point in the relatively-recent past and it started growing again.

I have the same question about why personnel who reach the third stage aren't just left there, though. Sounds like it would be much less wasteful than continuing to subject more musicians to the effect.

(Also, to the author, I appreciate the name of the Auseuil protocol.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this one. This SCP is another great example that things are not always black and white, especially concerning the Mekhanite-Sarkic conflict. The fact that a direct follower of Ion is intentionally trying to stop one of Yaldabaoth's Archons is in line with the views of the Proto-Sarkic belief; while being in direct contradiction to what the CotBG says the Sarkites should be doing.

Overall, a great skip with a lot of interesting implications. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this, especially the descriptions of the symptoms and the last lines of the journal. I do however have some questions:

All personnel in the second stage are to be redesignated as E-class personnel and should be considered permanently unfit for containment duties. All personnel that have reached the third stage must be terminated via brain trauma, then cremated.

Shouldn't this be in the Containment Procedures rather than in the description? Also, why doesn't the Foundation allow people who reach the third stage to keep playing, as they are nonetheless considered lost and their presence aids containment? Also, how was this thing contained before the Foundation reached it? Were there people remaining who had reached the third stage, from the Karcist's experiments? Also, why is this thing Euclid, when it's really hard to access and requires constant effort from the Foundation to contain and will most likely obliterate reality if it ever breaches containment?

Overall, I really enjoyed it, though the Karcist's descriptions of what they saw in the hole felt a bit cliche.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Points for reminding everyone that the Sarkic have change drastically. Proto-Sarkites were formed by a slave revolt who saw Yaldabaoth as a horror and who were willing to sacrifice the few for the many while Neo-Sarkicism are descended from aristocracy who believed in the sacrifice of the many for the few.

Also nice touch with the music, going back the the Azathoth inspiration.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one gave me tons of food for thought. It is incredibly refreshing to read about a Karcist who was moral, compassionate and dedicated to using his powers for good. And I loved the from-the-frontlines view of what seems to have been a later stage in the fall of the Daevite empire. There are some anomalies for which the Foundation really are the best guardians! Kudos and +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this article. Although I got a bit bored and confused with some of the more minuscule details of the anomaly, you had a pretty good hook ("deceased members should contain playing" really invested my interest") and a pretty good story behind the skip. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I saw "Erich Zahn" on it right away. Glad to see I was right about one of the inspirations.

I think it's good. On the other hand, though, I'm also getting somewhat tired of "balls of void" being everywhere nowadays.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed reading avout a Sarkic attempt to contain a dangerous anomaly. It's quite rare to see them portrayed as the good guys, and the change of role is quite novel. +1

item.title: This is a very good article that has some heavy-handedness (-1)
item.content:encoded:
I like the Sarkic element but I disagree with the execution of it. It's quite long, and perhaps unnecessarily so. Given its length, there's very little pushing the reader forward. I found myself stopping and getting bored after the third and fifth paragraphs of the main description. I had a similar issue with the log. (I also dislike that the log does not portray the ambiguous, awkward wording, or translator's notes as would be common in a translated text). It is because of these constant stoppings of boredom that I downvoted; the idea and the twist itself is strong and is a highlight of this article.

In order to improve this article, I would shorten it immensely- we only need to know the Void, the human sacrifice, the human-tissue walls, the playing, the document. The minutae about the exact nature of these can generally be handwaved so long as the strongest details- the gist of the concept, makes it into the reader's mind. Similarly, the document can be condensed and brightened. It spends a lot of time on opinions that can be reflected through the description of the events, and its most vivid moments (such as the gazing into the void, and the final sacrifice) does not have the strong lead-up and voice necessary.

What I mean by this is that although the most crucial moments in the document occur, they use pretty much the same tone and intensity as the beginning of the work. Readers naturally expect a tonal shift to match the change in the plot, and when it does not occur, the document as a whole feels one-note and the stakes here do not really feel like stakes.

I would like to emphasize: Your idea is excellent. Your execution is failing you. The execution can be worked on as you continue on your journey as a fiction writer. You have good taste. Please continue creating.(And let me read your drafts!)

Hit me up on IRC if you have any questions or concerns regarding this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great article! This was engrossing from beginning to end, and your style (both official parts and in the second addendum) is very easy on the eyes. I'm new, with only marginal knowledge of Sarkicism gained from skimming, and the way the concept was executed for this SCP is gonna send me reading everything in the Sarkic hub. LOL.

Anyway, congrats on such a nice first article. +1'd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this quite a bit. The Sarkic element was unexpected and added depth to the article. +1.

One question, why doesn't the foundation just get a few D-class to stage 3? Then they wouldn't have to worry about shifts and scheduling and such. Seems like a more surefire method of containment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, thanks for the kind words! Credit where credit's due, DarkStuff helped me rephrase that line to pack more of a punch.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: That's okay, no need to apologise. Thanks for the offer, anyway!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a kickass first article. This:

Deceased members of Tau-8 should be allowed to continue playing until the smell of decomposition becomes disruptive to other members.

is probably one of the best lines I've ever seen in the containment procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm also stoked to see this is a first article, and eagerly await more from the author. The documentation is sharp, crisp, and the language in the journals is archaic without getting tiring. Also dig how the journals wrapped a bow on the story without being overly expositional and convenient.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: Sorry I never got around to that crit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm surprised and happy to see that this is someone's first article. This is very quality!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So happy to see this finally complete and on the site. It's excellent, top to bottom. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, I like this scip a lot. But maybe put the addendum in a collapsible please? I got hit with a wall of text when I went down there.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Woo, my first SCP! After a few months, I'm finally confident enough to post it. A big thanks to Eskobar, DarkStuff, Meserach and Rounderhouse for their valuable critiques and suggestions!

Heavily inspired by the Sarkicism hub and the portrayal of Proto-Sarkicism here. Also inspired by SunnyClockwork's archon design, as well as H.P. Lovecraft's short story "The music of Erich Zann" and Lord Dunsany's "The Gods of Pegāna".

+ Image attributions
- Image attributions

First image: Source, Image is public domain, Edited by me
Second image: Source, License is CC-BY 3.0, Edited by me
